projects.thermite.title.text
site.hclose
site.br
projects.thermite.cautionary.1: Hot metal looks almost the same as cold metal, below a certain temperature. In daylight, metal that glows
incandescent red in the dark can look unremarkable, but looks can be deceiving and sometimes the 'feel' of
a thing can convey information about the item's average kinetic energy far better than its appearance can. To be
sure, due care is a far better substitute than the surprising realisation that, "YOW!!! THAT'S HOT!!!"
projects.thermite.cautionary.2: I'm reminded of an incident in one of my senior chemistry classes in high school. A classmate had been holding
his ruler in a blue bunsen flame for some time. After awhile, he put his hand up and asked, as he held the ruler
aloft, "Hey, Sir, how hot would I have to heat this before it melts?" The unsuspecting teacher reached towards
the ruler. It was a slow-motion train-wreck. It was inconceivable that the kid would not pull the ruler back.
With a yelp of pain, anger, surprise and ignominy, the teacher turned a funny shade of purple and started yelling.
Hot metal looks exactly like cold metal.
projects.thermite.cautionary.3: My crucibles tend to be made from steel. Occasionally, I've lined them with refractory to try to
prevent admixture of the molten metal with iron from the vessel. The steel is exposed to an oxidising
atmosphere, so it tends to rust.
projects.thermite.cautionary.4: I had acquired some metal disk caddies that were destined for landfill. It was late in the afternoon
when I arrived home from work and I thought I'd melt the metal dwn into ingots before dinner. I recall
thinking the metal was odd — very light, silver-gray, and with a plastic-like texture, but brittle.
All the warnings were there. (An acid test would have confirmed what I should have twigged to.
projects.thermite.cautionary.5: I broked the caddies up into pieces, loaded the crucible and waited for it to melt. Soon I realised that
there was more energy coming out of the crucible than I was putting in. "Ohhh, SHIT!!!"
(Enlightenment is not always well received but, sometimes, it can be life-changing.).
projects.thermite.cautionary.6: This was another of those times when I caused the problem, belatedly understood the danger, and took immediate,
though not risk free (my own life and limb) steps to ameliorate the problem. In this instance, the rusty steel
crucible coupled with the high magnesium aluminium alloy was a potential disaster. Magnesium-bearing alloys can
burn intensely once ignited and may react dangerously with water or oxidised contaminants.
projects.thermite.cautionary.7: I pulled the glowing crucible out of the furnace, dropped it onto a refractory block, then poured dry greensand
over it. Smoke was coming out of my 500mm high mountain of casting sand. I suddenly had my very own backyard
volcano — I was shitting cinder-blocks! For the next two hours, I carefully monitored and misted
my fiery backyard Vesivius to facilitate evaporative cooling, (being painfully aware of the danger and consequences
of too much water). I regained control.
projects.thermite.cautionary.8: I just don't find fire as exciting as when I was young.
